coredata.dc:title: A magnetic adsorbent of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, zinc metal-organic framework and molecularly imprinted polymer to extract phenylureas
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Sa-nguanprang, Surisa
Phuruangrat, Anukorn
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A highly selective composite nano-sorbent was designed and fabricated for the extraction and enrichment of phenylureas. The nano-sorbent was composed of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), a zinc metal-organic framework (IRMOF-1) and magnetite nanoparticles incorporated in molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The fabricated nano-sorbent was characterized and applied to extract and enrich three phenylureas in a dispersive magnetic solid-phase extraction (d-MSPE) process. The developed method was highly sensitive and detected the target phenylureas at trace levels. The linear range was from 1.0 to 150 µg L−1 and the limit of detection was 1.0 µg L−1. The large specific surface area of the nano-sorbent produced highly efficient adsorption. When applied to extract phenylureas in vegetable samples, recoveries were in the range of 80.2–95.8% with relative standard deviations lower than 8.0%. The developed nano-sorbent not only exhibited highly selective and efficient extraction but also good stability which can be reused four times, and ease of fabrication. The developed strategy can also be modified for the extraction and determination of other organic compounds in various complex samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent (A) and the d-MSPE procedure for the extraction of phenylureas (B).

The SEM images of IRMOF-1 are at 50X (A) and 5000X (B). The SEM images of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP are at 12000X (C) and 50000X (D). TEM images of N-GQDs (E) and GQDs/MIP sorbent (F). Adsorption-desorption isotherm was plotted for the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP (G) and the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/NIP (H) nano-sorbents.
Extraction recoveries of different composite adsorbents for the determination of phenylureas (n = 3).
HPLC chromatograms of soybean milk (a), a spiked sample (b) and a standard solution of three PUHs (c) extracted using the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent.
Analytical results for the extraction and determination of metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron using the composite N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-adsorbent (n = 3) and analysis by HPLC-DAD.
Comparison of the proposed method with other methods for the determination of phenylureas.

head.ce:title: A magnetic adsorbent of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, zinc metal-organic framework and molecularly imprinted polymer to extract phenylureas

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective composite nano-sorbent was designed and fabricated for the extraction and enrichment of phenylureas. The nano-sorbent was composed of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), a zinc metal-organic framework (IRMOF-1) and magnetite nanoparticles incorporated in molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The fabricated nano-sorbent was characterized and applied to extract and enrich three phenylureas in a dispersive magnetic solid-phase extraction (d-MSPE) process. The developed method was highly sensitive and detected the target phenylureas at trace levels. The linear range was from 1.0 to 150 µg L−1 and the limit of detection was 1.0 µg L−1. The large specific surface area of the nano-sorbent produced highly efficient adsorption. When applied to extract phenylureas in vegetable samples, recoveries were in the range of 80.2–95.8% with relative standard deviations lower than 8.0%. The developed nano-sorbent not only exhibited highly selective and efficient extraction but also good stability which can be reused four times, and ease of fabrication. The developed strategy can also be modified for the extraction and determination of other organic compounds in various complex samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenylurea herbicides (PUHs) are widely used in agriculture to control a wide range of weeds and grasses pre- and post-emergence (Wang et al., 2020). PUHs can remain in the environment for a long time after entering the groundwater. Their bioaccumulation has caused concern since PUHs such as metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron have demonstrated endocrine disruption and genotoxic or ecotoxic effects (Marlatt and Martyniuk, 2017). According to European Union (EU) regulations, the maximum limits in drinking water and foodstuffs are 0.10 µg L−1 and 0.2–2.0 mg kg−1, respectively (Fenoll et al., 2012). The development of a simple and sensitive analytical method for the quantitative determination of PUHs in environmental and agricultural products is a necessity.

High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is the conventional method of determining PUHs in soil (Langeron et al., 2014), crops (Li et al., 2016), food (Han et al., 2021) and water samples (Saraji et al., 2020). Typically, PUHs are found in real samples at low residual concentrations, coexisting with various matrix interferences. Consequently, sample pretreatment and enrichment are essential to eliminate matrix interferences and ensure the accuracy and precision of analysis. Pretreatment methods for the extraction of PUHs include solid-phase extraction (SPE) (Marín et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2022), solid-phase microextraction (SPME) (Mughari et al., 2007), liquid-phase microextraction (LPME) (Mughari et al., 2010) and dispersive magnetic solid-phase extraction (d-MSPE) techniques (Tong et al., 2019). d-MSPE is effective because an external magnet can be used to separate the sorbent from the sample solution without filtration or centrifugation (Rahimpoor et al., 2022). The extraction efficiency of d-MSPE depends on the sorbent, which must be carefully designed and fabricated (Wang et al., 2023a; Wang et al., 2023b).

The selectivity of d-MSPE sorbents can be improved by the use of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). These artificial receptors are synthesized using a combination of functional monomer and crosslinking agents with a template that creates adsorption sites that interact only with the target molecule (Fan et al., 2016). The selection of an appropriate magnetic carrier is an important consideration. MIPs can be imprinted on the surface of silica-coated magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4 @SiO2) to produce multiple recognition sites, which significantly improve adsorption (Sheykhaghaei et al., 2016). To enhance adsorption performance, affinity materials can be added to form a composite sorbent. An interesting affinity material for the extraction of metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron is a zinc metalorganic framework (IRMOF-1). IRMOF-1 has a large surface area with homogenous open cavities and contains functional groups of NH2, CO, CHO, COOH, and OH on the pores and outer surface that promote the adsorption of analytes through hydrogen bonding (Bazargan et al., 2021). New approaches to surface imprinting involve carbon-based materials. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are widely used owing to their unique nanostructures and large specific surface areas (Liu et al., 2018). Furthermore, N-GQDs act as electron donors, facilitating the deprotonation of target analytes and their surface presents π-π conjugate rings, hydroxyls and carboxyl group that can form π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding with target analytes (Rao et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017).

In this work, a composite nano-sorbent was designed and fabricated for the first time for the extraction and enrichment of metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron. The nano-adsorbent was synthesized by a sol-gel polymerization process using Fe3O4 @SiO2 as the supporting matrix for a MIP layer composited with N-GQDs and IRMOF-1 (N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP). N-GQDs are used to improve the recognition of template molecules during the printing process because they contain π-π conjugate ring, hydroxyl and carboxyl group which can form π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding with target analytes. IRMOF-1 consists of a homogenous open cavity and a large surface area that can improve the high extraction of the composite. While the SiO2 coating on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles can enhance the stability and dispersibility of the adsorbent. The composite nano-sorbent was characterized and the parameters influencing extraction efficiency were evaluated and optimized. Finally, metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron, extracted from food samples with the proposed sorbent, were quantitatively determined using HPLC-DAD.

Metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron were from the Laboratory of the Government Chemist (Augsburg, Germany). Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate, iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris-HMA), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and ammonia solution 25% were from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Terephthalic acid (TPA) was from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Sodium hydroxide was from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Citric acid and zinc nitrate hexahydrate were from Elago Enterprises Pty Ltd. (KemAus, Australia). Dimethylformamide, trichloromethane and methanol were from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand).

The chromatographic analysis was performed on a Hewlett-Packard 1100 combined with a diode array detector (DAD) (Agilent Technologies Inc., USA). The target analytes (metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron) were separated by isocratic elution using a C18 analytical column (150 mm × 4.6 mm I.D., 5.0 µm particle size, Fortis Technologies Ltd., UK). The mobile phase was a mixture of ultrapure water (line A) and acetonitrile (line B) (55:45, v/v) at a constant flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The DAD monitoring wavelength was set at 244 nm. The sample injection volume was 20.0 µL. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (FEI Quanta 400, ThermoScientific, Italy), transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL JEM 2010, Japan) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) (PerkinElmer, USA) were applied to observe morphologies and evaluate the structural properties of the nanocomposite adsorbent. Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were plotted (ASAP2460, Micromeritics, USA) to measure the specific surface area and porosity of the sorbent. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (TGA8000, PerkinElmer, USA) was utilized to determine the stability of the composite nano-sorbent. CHNS/O analyzer (Flash 2000, ThermoScientifc, Italy) was used to investigate the carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen contents of the nano-sorbent. Zeta potentials were obtained by Zeta potential analyzer (Model Zeta PALS, Brookhaven, USA). The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns were recorded using an XRD analyzer (Empreyan, PAN analytical, Netherlands).

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by co-precipitation. A precursor solution was prepared by mixing 9.40 g of FeCl3.6 H2O and 3.40 g of FeCl2.4 H2O with 160 mL deionized water (DI) in a three-necked flask. The solution was stirred at 500 rpm and heated to 80 °C under a N2 gas flow. At this temperature, 20.0 mL of NH4OH was added. A dark precipitate of Fe3O4 nanoparticles was produced. Stirring continued for 1 h. The synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles were washed three times with 50.0 mL of DI water and then dried at 80 °C for 6 h. Fe3O4 @SiO2 nanoparticles were obtained by coating the synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles with silica by sol-gel polymerization (Orachorn and Bunkoed, 2022). In a mixture solution of 100 mL of ethanol and 50.0 mL of DI water, 2.00 g of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed and 2.0 mL of ammonia solution (25%v/v) were added to the dispersion. The dispersion was heated to 40 °C and 2.0 mL of TEOS were slowly added to the mixture. After stirring at 300 rpm for 12 h, Fe3O4 @SiO2 nanoparticles were collected using a magnet, washed repeatedly three times with 10 mL of ethanol and DI water, and then dried for 6 h at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were prepared in a one-step pyrolysis of 0.20 g citric acid and 0.50 g Tris-HMA in a 50.0 mL round-bottom flask at 225 °C for 15 min. The reaction was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and 1.0 M of sodium hydroxide solution was gradually added to produce a solution at pH 7.0. The neutralized N-GQDs solution was diluted to a volume of 50.0 mL with DI water (Lin et al., 2015).

The synthesis of IRMOF-1 was modified from a previous report (Isobe et al., 2017). IRMOF-1 was synthesized by the direct solvothermal reaction of 0.37 g of Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O and 0.17 g of terephthalic acid in the presence of 20.0 mL of DMF solution. The reactants were placed in a stainless-steel autoclave reactor and heated at 105 °C for 24 h. The autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature. The precipitate was filtered, washed and immersed in 10.0 mL of trichloromethane to allow the exchange with the solvent. The solvent was refreshed every 24 h. Finally, the precipitate was filtered and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain IRMOF-1 powder.

The composite MIP nano-sorbent was synthesized by a sol-gel method using metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron as template molecules. The template molecules were dissolved in 30.0 mL of methanol in a brown bottle and 357.0 µL of APTES were added as a functional monomer. After 1 h, 40.0 mL of N-GQDs, 0.80 g of Fe3O4 @SiO2 and 0.50 g of IRMOF-1 were added to the mixture solution and stirred for 1 h. Then, 2.63 mL of TEOS, as a cross-linking agent, and 900 µL of ammonia solution, as an initiator, were added. The mixture was stirred at 500 rpm for 24 h at room temperature to complete the polymerization process. After polymerization, the template molecules were removed by washing with methanol until no template could be detected in the washing solution by HPLC. The MIP nano-sorbent was dried at 60 °C for 24 h. A composite non-imprinted polymer (N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/NIP) nano-sorbent was also synthesized using the same procedure but without the template molecules. The synthesis procedure of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent is illustrated in Fig. 1A.

The d-MSPE procedure for the extraction of PUHs using the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent is shown in Fig. 1B. The target analytes were extracted for 20 min from 10.0 mL of standard or sample solution in a glass vial containing 0.20 g of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent with stirring at 1000 rpm. After extraction, an external magnet was placed at the bottom of the glass vial to collect the magnetic composite sorbent, and the supernatant was discarded. The target analytes were desorbed from the sorbent for 30 min in 3.00 mL of ethyl acetate in a glass vial. The sorbent was isolated with an external magnet and the eluate was dried using a rotary vacuum evaporator at 50 °C. Finally, the residue containing the target PUHs was re-dissolved in 1.00 mL of mobile phase and filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter before HPLC analysis.

To optimize analytical performances, the effects of the amount of IRMOF-1, amount of Fe3O4 @SiO2, ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker, extraction time, desorption solvent type, desorption solvent volume, desorption time, sample stirring rate, sample volume, sample pH and sample ionic strength were studied. The effect of each parameter was evaluated based on the extraction recovery which was calculated as follows: extraction recovery (%) = (CV/CxVx) × 100, where C and Cx are the concentration of PUHs in the reconstituted solvent (μg L−1) and spiked sample, and V and Vx are the volume of the reconstituted solvent (1.0 mL) and the spiked sample, respectively.

Cucumber, tomato, and radish samples were cut into small pieces and homogenized. 50.0 g of homogenized sample were placed in a 50.0 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred into a volumetric flask and diluted to 100 mL with DI water. After that, the sample solution was filtered and used for the extraction using the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent (Yuan et al., 2020).

A 50.0 mL volume of soybean milk was placed in a centrifuge tube and 0.50 g of trichloroacetic acid was added to precipitate proteins and remove lipids in soybean milk. The solution was sonicated for 15 min and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. After collecting the supernatant, the precipitate was vortex-washed with 5.0 mL of methanol for 5 min and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, all the supernatant was combined, filtered and used for the extraction using the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-adsorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the sorbent and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. S1A) presented an absorption peak of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations at 3343 cm−1 that confirmed the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups. The strong absorption peaks at 1669 and 1559 cm−1 were attributed to the CO of the carbonyl group and skeletal vibration of the aromatic ring, respectively. The absorption peak at 1417 cm−1 corresponded to the C–N stretching vibration. The Fe3O4 @SiO2 spectrum (Fig. S1B) presented absorption peaks of N-H/O-H, CC and C-OH stretching at 3398, 1630 and 1403 cm−1, respectively, and peaks at 1075 and 590 cm−1 corresponding to Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching vibration and Fe-O stretching vibration, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of IRMOF-1 (Fig. S1C) presented absorption peaks of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations at 3500–2700 cm−1. Absorption peaks at 1606 and 1505 cm−1 were attributed to C-O stretching vibration and the peak at 547 cm−1 indicated the Zn-O vibration of the tetrahedral coordinated Zn4O cluster. The FT-IR spectrum of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent (Fig. S1D) presented similar absorption peaks from about 3400–1406 cm−1 to the spectrum of N-GQDs, and similar absorption peaks at 1092 and 590 cm−1 to the spectrum of Fe3O4 @SiO2. In addition, the absorption peak at 570 cm−1 was attributed to Zn-O vibration in IRMOF-1. The presence of absorption peaks of N-GQDs, Fe3O4 @SiO2 and IRMOF-1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent indicated the successful synthesis of the composite.

The N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent was further characterized by using elemental analysis to determine carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen contents. Nitrogen content was 1.84%, carbon content 5.34%, hydrogen content 2.26% and oxygen content 8.77% (Fig. S2). These results confirmed the successful synthesis of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent.

The synthesized IRMOF-1 exhibited an ordered crystalline structure in the 2ϴ range of 5–25° (Fig. S3A). The peak positions in particular diffraction angles (2ϴ∼9.6°, ∼12.6°, ∼14.8°, ∼17.0° and ∼19.5°) exhibited a good agreement with the distinctive structure of the IRMOF-1. The X-ray diffractogram (XRD) of Fe3O4 and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP particles are shown in Fig. S3B. The diffraction peaks with 2θ values of 30.2, 35.6, 43.4, 53.7, 57.2, and 62.7° correspond to the crystal planes (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) of the crystalline Fe3O4 phase, these match with the standard data JCPDS card nos. 01–084–2782 for Fe3O4. N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent exhibited magnetic properties, as evidenced by the XRD pattern of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP, which remains the same crystal structure of Fe3O4. The diffraction peak of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP adsorbents decreased due to obstruction of the polymer layer.

The thermal stability of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent was evaluated by TGA. The thermogram showed a small weight loss of about 8.42% from 25° to 100°C, which was due to the removal of physically and chemically adsorbed water molecules (Fig. S4). From 100° to 450°C, a weight loss of about 7.25% occurred due to the degradation of the polymer layer of the composite. Above 450 °C, a weight loss of 3.35% was attributed to the loss of other substances on the surface of Fe3O4 @SiO2. These results confirmed that the thermal stability of the fabricated nano-sorbent was suitable for analysis of the target analytes at room temperature (30 ± 5 °C).

The morphologies of IRMOF-1 and the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent were examined by SEM. IRMOF-1 was composed of cubic crystal particles with an average size of about 100–200 µm ( Fig. 2A). The surface of IRMOF-1 presented sheets or platelets stacked at various angles (Fig. 2B). The fabricated nano-sorbent displayed uniformly spherical shaped nanoparticles (Fig. 2C and D). The surface of the nano-sorbent was rough, resulting in a large surface area that could enhance the adsorption of target analytes. The TEM images of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP particles are demonstrated in Fig. 2E and F. The average particle size of N-GQDs is about 4–5 nm and the results indicated that the N-GQDs were incorporated into the MIP layer.

The surface area and porosity of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/NIP nano-sorbents were evaluated by plotting N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 2G and H). Both isotherms were type IV curves with an H3 hysteresis loop, indicating mesoporous materials according to IUPAC guidelines. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface areas of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/NIP nano-sorbents were 255.67 m2 g−1 and 104.36 m2 g−1, respectively. The mesoporous structure and large surface area of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent ensured sufficient active adsorption sites and rapid transfer for good adsorption performances.

Zeta potential was also measured in DI water to determine the stability of the colloidal solution. The N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP displayed a characteristic negative charge (−40.4 mV) due to the abundant amino, carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-GQDs and Fe3O4 @SiO2. The negative charges prevent the accumulation of particles and improve the stability of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nanoparticles. When the PUHs standard solution was added into the solution containing N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nanoparticles, the zeta potential value changed from − 40.4 mV to − 43.3 mV, which demonstrated the apparent interaction between the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP and target PUHs (Li et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sorbent fabrication and extraction condition

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the amount of IRMOF-1 used for the fabrication of the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP sorbent was investigated at 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, 70.0 and 100.0 mg. Extraction efficiency was low when less than 50.0 mg was used since there were few adsorption sites for target analytes (Fig. S5A). Extraction efficiency decreased when more than 50.0 mg was used since excess IRMOF-1 blocked recognition sites. Therefore, 50.0 mg of IRMOF-1 was used in the fabrication of the nano-sorbent.

The effect of the amount of Fe3O4 @SiO2 used for the preparation of the nano-sorbent was investigated at 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, 75.0 and 100.0 mg. Extraction efficiency was low when less than 75.0 mg of Fe3O4 @SiO2 was used because it was difficult to separate the nano-sorbent from the sample solution. Extraction efficiency was also low when more than 75.0 mg was used because the nano-sorbent formed agglomerations (Fig. S5B). Therefore, 75.0 mg of Fe3O4 @SiO2 was used to fabricate the nano-sorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker applied for the synthesis of MIP was optimized. The template-to-monomer ratio was varied at 1:37, 1:42 and 1:47 and the template-to-crosslinker ratio at 1:55, 1:60 and 1:65. Extraction efficiency was highest at a ratio of template-to-monomer-to-crosslinker of 1:42:60. When the proportion of crosslinker (TEOS) was lower than 60, the number of cross-links formed was limited, resulting in fewer recognition sites in the MIP. When the proportion of cross-linker was higher than 60, extraction efficiency decreased because the generated polymer was rigid and bound less easily with target analytes. When the proportion of monomer (APTES) was lower than 42, extraction efficiency decreased because the number of template–monomer interactions was reduced, limiting the formation of recognition sites. When the ratio of monomer was higher than 42, extraction efficiency decreased because excess monomer disturbed interactions with the template molecules during polymerization, again limiting the formation of specific recognition sites. The optimal ratio for the fabrication of the composite nano-sorbent was 1:42:60 (Fig. S5C).

To optimize the desorption of the three PUHs, the desorption solvent type, desorption solvent volume and desorption time were carefully investigated. Methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone, ethyl acetate and methanol: acetic acid (1.0%w/v) were used to desorb the extracted PUHs. Ethyl acetate provided better extraction efficiency than the other solvents (Fig. S6A). The volume of ethyl acetate was then investigated from 1.0 to 10.0 mL. The results showed that the peak areas of three PUHs remained unchanged when the volume of ethyl acetate was over 3.0 mL, indicating that 3.0 mL of ethyl acetate was sufficient to completely desorb the PUHs from the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent (Fig. S6B).

The effect of desorption time was investigated from 5 to 40 min (Fig. S6C). The three PUHs were completely desorbed within 30 min and no obvious increases in peak areas were observed by increasing the desorption time to 40 min. Therefore, the desorption time of 30 min was used for further experiments.

To increase the mass transfer rate and reduce the time needed to reach adsorption equilibrium, the stirring rate was optimized from 500 to 1500 rpm (Fig. S6D). Extraction efficiency was low when the stirring rate was slower than 1000 rpm since the sorbent and the sample solution were not sufficiently homogenized. Extraction efficiency was significantly increased with stirring in the range of 1000–1500 rpm. The increase in the stirring rate improved the mass transfer of PUHs to the sorbent and decreased the time to reach extraction equilibrium. Therefore, the optimal stirring rate of 1000 rpm was selected for further experiments.

To ensure that contact between the nano-sorbent and the target analytes was sufficient for optimal recoveries, extraction time was investigated from 10 to 50 min. Extraction efficiency increased with extraction time to 20 min (Fig. S7A). Extending extraction time further resulted in slight decreases in extraction efficiency, perhaps due to the desorption of analytes from the nano-sorbent into the solution. Therefore, 20 min was selected as the optimal extraction time.

The effect of solution ionic strength was evaluated by adding NaCl at concentrations of 0.0%, 1.0%, 3.0%, 5.0% and 10.0%w/v (Fig. S7B). Extraction efficiency decreased with increasing NaCl concentration because the increased viscosity of the sample solution disrupted the extraction process. Therefore, there was no need to add NaCl to the sample solution.

The influence of sample solution pH can affect both the adsorbent and target analytes. In this study, sample solution pH was varied at 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0 and 11.0 (Fig. S7C) by adjusting with 1.0 M of hydrochloric acid (HCl) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solutions. Extraction efficiency was best at pH 5.0–7.0. At pH lower than 5.0, the surface of the sorbent was positively charged and the target PUHs became protonated, decreasing extraction efficiency. At pH higher than 7.0, PUHs were deprotonated and the carboxyl groups on the imprinted silica layer of the adsorbent decomposed, reducing hydrogen bonding between PUHs and the sorbent. Since the pH of the real samples was between 5.0 and 7.0, there was no need to adjust the sample solution pH in the remaining experiments.

The effect of sample volume was investigated at 5.0, 10.0, 15.0 and 20.0 mL (Fig. S7D). Extraction efficiency decreased with a sample volume of more than 10.0 mL because the active sites of the sorbent were saturated and PUHs could not bind to the active sites of the sorbent. Therefore, 10.0 mL of sample solution was selected for further experiments. The optimal conditions are summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative performance study of composite sorbents
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the extraction performance of the developed sorbent, the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent was applied to extract the target PUHs and the results were compared with those of N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/NIP, N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/MIP, and N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/NIP nano-sorbents. The N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent produced the highest extraction recoveries for all three PUHs ( Fig. 3). This result confirmed that both MIP and IRMOF-1 can improve the extraction ability of the composite sorbent. The MIP presented specific imprinted sites to bind with the three PUHs and IRMOF-1 enhanced hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between the target analytes and the composite sorbent. Therefore, the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent was applied to extract and determine PUHs in real samples.

The performance of the developed method for the extraction and determination of the three PUHs in food samples was evaluated under the optimal experimental conditions. Linear ranges, limits of detection (LODs) and limits of quantification (LOQs) were determined (Table S2). The linear range was investigated using matrix-matched spiked samples. Standard solutions of different concentrations of the three PUHs were added to the sample solution. The target analytes were extracted using the developed method (n = 3) and determined by HPLC-DAD. The linear ranges obtained for metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron were in the concentration range of 1.0–150.0 µg L−1 with coefficients of determination (R2) greater than 0.99. The results indicated a wide linear range and good linearity. The LODs (signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3) and LOQs (signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 10) were 1.0 µg L−1 and 3.0 µg L−1, respectively. These results indicated the sensitivity of the developed method toward the determination of metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron. Enrichment factors (EF) were calculated from the following equation: EF = C f /C i , where C f is the concentration of the extracted PUHs calculated from calibration curves (μg L−1) and C i is the initial PUHs concentration in the sample (μg L−1). EFs were evaluated using 10.0 mL of sample solution spiked with the three PUHs at 50.0 μg L−1. EFs ranged from 9.2 to 9.5. The enrichment factors of the developed method can be improved by increasing sample volume and some optimization parameters may be required. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) varied from 3.2% to 4.8%, indicating the good precision of the developed method.

To investigate the reusability of the developed nano-sorbent, the used sorbent was washed with 3.0 mL of methanol by stirring for at least 10 min. If no target analyte was detected in the solvent after 10 min, the nano-sorbent was reused for the next extraction. The developed nano-sorbent could be used for up to 4 cycles with good recoveries (Fig. S8). Extraction efficiency decreased after 4 cycles; perhaps due to damage suffered by the recognition sites during repeated desorption and washing. Therefore, the developed nano-sorbent was reused four times to help reduce the consumption of chemical reagents, analysis costs and fabrication time.

The performance of the developed nano-sorbent was compared with the performance of a commercial SPE cartridge. The results showed that the extraction recoveries of both sorbents were above 80% (Fig. S9). However, the %recoveries of the proposed nano-sorbent were higher since it had more specific adsorption sites to extract the target analytes. Also, extraction with the proposed nano-sorbent can be performed without an SPE manifold system and does not produce backpressure since the nano-sorbent is dispersed in the sample solution. Therefore, the proposed method could be an effective alternative for the extraction and determination of PUHs in real samples.

The kinetics of adsorption of the three target PUHs on the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent were investigated by adding 0.20 g of the composite nano-adsorbent into 10.0 mL of sample solution containing 100.0 μg L−1 of each PUH. The adsorption mechanisms of the PUHs on sorbent were evaluated according to the following equations (Surapong and Burakham, 2021):

Pseudo first-order equation:
ln(Qe–Qt) = lnQe – K1t

Pseudo second order equation:
t/Qt = 1/(k2Qe2) + t/Qt
where Qe and Qt are the amount of PUHs (mg g−1) absorbed at equilibrium (h) and any time t (h), respectively. The pseudo-first-order rate (k1) and pseudo-second-order rate (k2) were calculated from the plots of ln(Qe–Qt) versus t, and t/Qt versus t, respectively. The coefficients of determination (R2) of the pseudo-second order model were greater than those of the pseudo-first order model (Table S3). The better fitting of the data to the pseudo-second order model indicated that the adsorption of the target PUHs on the proposed nano-sorbent was a chemical process, driven by the formation of hydrogen bonds between PUHs and hydroxyl groups in the cavities of the nano-sorbent.

Adsorption isotherms were also studied to understand the interaction of the three target PUHs with the proposed nano-adsorbent. Isothermal adsorption was studied by changing the concentration of three PUHs from 0.5 to 150.0 μg L−1. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were applied using the following equations:

Langmuir equation:
1/Qe = 1/Ce(1/QmaxKL) + 1/Qmax

Freundlich:
lnQ = (1/n)lnCe + lnKF
where Ce (μg L−1) is the equilibrium concentration of three PUHs in solution, Qmax is the maximum saturated adsorption capacity (μg g−1), KL is the Langmuir rate constant of (L mg−1), KF is the Freundlich rate constant (μg g−1) and n is a heterogeneity parameter (Liu et al., 2022).

The results revealed that the adsorption of the three PUHs increased gradually with increments of their concentration (Table S4). When the concentration was 50.0 μg L−1, the adsorption capacity reached the maximum saturated adsorption capacity of 1.0603, 0.7766 and 0.7844 μg g−1 for metoxuron, chlorotoluron and monolinuron, respectively. The correlation coefficient (R2) of the Langmuir isotherm was higher than the R2 of the Freundlich isotherm (Shah et al., 2018). Therefore, the Langmuir model better explained the studied adsorption process, implying monolayer adsorption (Jiang et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2023).

The proposed method was applied to determine the three PUHs in tomato, cucumber, radish and soybean milk. The accuracy of the method was evaluated in terms of relative recoveries, calculated from the equation relative recovery (%) = (CF-CR)/CS x100, where CF is the total concentration of PUHs found in the spiked sample, CR is the original concentration of PUHs in the sample and CS is the standard concentration of PUHs spiked in the sample. The accuracy of the developed method was evaluated in terms of recoveries. Tomato, cucumber, radish, and soybean milk samples were spiked with standards of three PUHs at 20.0, 50.0 and 100.0 μg kg−1 and then extracted and determined by using the developed method. The relative recoveries of the three PUHs were in the range of 80.2–94.6% with RSDs between 1.4% and 6.2% from tomato samples, 80.7–94.8% with RSD between 1.2% and 6.0% from cucumber samples, 81.9–95.6% with RSD between 1.0% and 8.0% from radish samples and 80.9–95.8% with RSD between 1.0% and 5.8% from soybean milk samples ( Table 1) which were acceptable by the AOAC guidelines (AOAC, 2016). The error of analytical results of this method may be due to systematic errors. The relative recoveries confirmed the good accuracy of the developed method, which can be reliably used for the determination of trace PUHs in tomato, cucumber, radish and soybean milk samples. The typical chromatograms of a real sample, a spiked sample extracted with the N-GQDs/Fe3O4 @SiO2/IRMOF-1/MIP nano-sorbent and a standard solution of the three target analytes are shown in Fig. 4. The matrix effects were also evaluated by comparing the slope of the calibration curve in sample matrices with that in the solvent (Jiang et al., 2023). The developed method exhibited the matrix effects ranging from 2.9% to 9.1% (Table S5). To ensure an accurate result, matrix-matched calibration curves were used for quantification of the PUHs in real samples.

The analytical performance parameters of the proposed method were compared with the performance parameters of the reported methods ( Table 2). In terms of linear range and LOD, the proposed method was superior to reported methods that used a similar detector. In addition, the developed method showed good selectivity toward the target analytes and reusability for up to four extraction-desorption cycles. The comparison confirmed that the proposed method is easy to perform, simple to operate, accurate, sensitive, and efficiently determine PUHs.

A highly selective and effective nano-sorbent was designed and fabricated for the extraction of phenylurea herbicides. The nano-sorbent was prepared in a sol-gel polymerization method by integrating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, silica-modified magnetite nanoparticles and a zinc metal-organic framework into molecularly imprinted polymer. The nano-sorbent was used in a dispersive magnetic solid-phase extraction (d-MSPE) coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) for quantitative analysis of trace three PUHs in food samples. The nano-adsorbent demonstrated high adsorption capacity toward PUHs via hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking and exhibited high specific surface areas which helped to enhance adsorption ability. The extraction of the target molecules with the fabricated nano-adsorbent was highly efficient and selective. The adsorbent was simple to prepare, and easy to use, and reproducibility and stability were good. The developed composite adsorbent exhibited can be used as an alternative adsorbent for the extraction of phenylureas in various samples. The composite adsorbent has also a potential application for the extraction and enrichment of other compounds in various samples.

Phuruangrat Anukorn: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Sa-nguanprang Surisa: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft.
